title: Pandas Intro
content:
Pandas Introduction

What is Pandas?

Pandas is a powerful open-source data manipulation and analysis library for Python. It provides data structures like Series and DataFrame for handling structured data.

Why Use Pandas?

Pandas makes it easy to handle tabular data, perform data cleaning, analysis, and visualization. It's built on top of NumPy and is the go-to tool for data science in Python.

Example
import pandas as pd

# Create a simple DataFrame
data = {'Name': ['John', 'Anna', 'Peter'],
        'Age': [25, 30, 35]}
df = pd.DataFrame(data)
print(df)

title: Pandas Series
content:
Pandas Series

What is a Series?

A Pandas Series is a one-dimensional labeled array capable of holding any data type. It's similar to a column in a spreadsheet or a Python list.

Creating Series

Series can be created from lists, dictionaries, or NumPy arrays.

Series Examples
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# From a list
s = pd.Series([1, 2, 3, 4, 5])
print(s)

# With custom index
s = pd.Series([10, 20, 30], index=['a', 'b', 'c'])
print(s)

# From a dictionary
s = pd.Series({'a': 1, 'b': 2, 'c': 3})
print(s)

# From NumPy array
arr = np.array([1, 2, 3, 4])
s = pd.Series(arr)
print(s)

title: Pandas DataFrame
content:
Pandas DataFrame

What is a DataFrame?

A DataFrame is a two-dimensional labeled data structure with columns of potentially different types. It's similar to a spreadsheet or SQL table.

Creating DataFrames

DataFrames can be created from dictionaries, lists, or by reading files.

DataFrame Examples
import pandas as pd

# From dictionary
data = {'Name': ['John', 'Anna', 'Peter'],
        'Age': [25, 30, 35],
        'City': ['NYC', 'LA', 'Chicago']}
df = pd.DataFrame(data)
print(df)

# From list of dictionaries
data = [{'Name': 'John', 'Age': 25},
        {'Name': 'Anna', 'Age': 30},
        {'Name': 'Peter', 'Age': 35}]
df = pd.DataFrame(data)
print(df)

# Empty DataFrame
df = pd.DataFrame()
print(df)

title: Reading Data
content:
Reading Data with Pandas

Reading CSV Files

Pandas can read various file formats. CSV is the most common format for data analysis.

Other File Formats

Pandas supports Excel, JSON, HTML, SQL, and more.

Reading Files
import pandas as pd

# Read CSV
df = pd.read_csv('data.csv')
print(df.head())

# Read Excel
df = pd.read_excel('data.xlsx')
print(df.head())

# Read JSON
df = pd.read_json('data.json')
print(df.head())

# Read HTML tables
dfs = pd.read_html('http://example.com')
df = dfs[0]

# Read SQL
# df = pd.read_sql('SELECT * FROM table', connection)

title: Data Selection
content:
Data Selection in Pandas

Selecting Columns

You can select one or multiple columns from a DataFrame using bracket notation or dot notation.

Selecting Rows

Use loc (label-based) or iloc (integer-based) for row selection.

Selection Examples
import pandas as pd

df = pd.DataFrame({
    'Name': ['John', 'Anna', 'Peter'],
    'Age': [25, 30, 35],
    'City': ['NYC', 'LA', 'Chicago']
})

# Select single column
print(df['Name'])
print(df.Name)

# Select multiple columns
print(df[['Name', 'Age']])

# Select rows by label
print(df.loc[0])

# Select rows by integer position
print(df.iloc[0])

# Select specific value
print(df.loc[0, 'Name'])

title: Filtering Data
content:
Filtering Data in Pandas

Boolean Filtering

Filter rows based on conditions using boolean masks.

Multiple Conditions

Combine multiple conditions using & (and) or | (or) operators.

Filtering Examples
import pandas as pd

df = pd.DataFrame({
    'Name': ['John', 'Anna', 'Peter', 'Mike'],
    'Age': [25, 30, 35, 28],
    'City': ['NYC', 'LA', 'Chicago', 'NYC']
})

# Single condition
print(df[df['Age'] > 28])

# Multiple conditions
print(df[(df['Age'] > 25) & (df['City'] == 'NYC')])

# Using isin()
print(df[df['City'].isin(['NYC', 'LA'])])

# Using str.contains()
df2 = pd.DataFrame({
    'Name': ['John', 'Anna', 'Peter']
})
print(df2[df2['Name'].str.contains('oh')])

title: Data Cleaning
content:
Data Cleaning with Pandas

Handling Missing Data

Pandas uses NaN to represent missing values. Use isna(), dropna(), or fillna() to handle them.

Removing Duplicates

Use drop_duplicates() to remove duplicate rows.

Cleaning Examples
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

df = pd.DataFrame({
    'Name': ['John', 'Anna', 'John', 'Peter'],
    'Age': [25, np.nan, 35, 28],
    'City': ['NYC', 'LA', 'NYC', 'Chicago']
})

# Check for missing values
print(df.isna())
print(df.isna().sum())

# Drop rows with missing values
print(df.dropna())

# Fill missing values
print(df.fillna(0))
print(df['Age'].fillna(df['Age'].mean()))

# Remove duplicates
print(df.drop_duplicates())

title: Data Transformation
content:
Data Transformation in Pandas

Applying Functions

Use apply(), map(), or applymap() to transform data.

String Operations

Pandas provides vectorized string operations through the .str accessor.

Transformation Examples
import pandas as pd

df = pd.DataFrame({
    'Name': ['john', 'anna', 'PETER'],
    'Age': [25, 30, 35]
})

# Apply function to column
df['Age_double'] = df['Age'].apply(lambda x: x * 2)
print(df)

# String operations
df['Name_upper'] = df['Name'].str.upper()
df['Name_lower'] = df['Name'].str.lower()
df['Name_len'] = df['Name'].str.len()

# Replace values
df['Name'] = df['Name'].str.replace('john', 'John')
print(df)

title: Grouping Data
content:
Grouping Data in Pandas

GroupBy Operation

The groupby() function allows you to group data and perform aggregate operations.

Aggregate Functions

Use sum(), mean(), count(), min(), max() on grouped data.

GroupBy Examples
import pandas as pd

df = pd.DataFrame({
    'Name': ['John', 'Anna', 'John', 'Peter', 'Anna'],
    'Department': ['Sales', 'IT', 'Sales', 'IT', 'Sales'],
    'Salary': [50000, 60000, 55000, 65000, 58000]
})

# Group by department
grouped = df.groupby('Department')
print(grouped.sum())

# Group by multiple columns
print(df.groupby(['Department', 'Name']).sum())

# Multiple aggregations
print(df.groupby('Department').agg({
    'Salary': ['sum', 'mean', 'count']
}))

title: Merging Data
content:
Merging Data in Pandas

Merge

Use merge() to combine DataFrames like SQL joins.

Concat & Join

Use concat() to stack DataFrames and join() for index-based merging.

Merge Examples
import pandas as pd

df1 = pd.DataFrame({
    'ID': [1, 2, 3],
    'Name': ['John', 'Anna', 'Peter']
})

df2 = pd.DataFrame({
    'ID': [1, 2, 3],
    'Salary': [50000, 60000, 55000]
})

# Merge
result = pd.merge(df1, df2, on='ID')
print(result)

# Concat
df3 = pd.DataFrame({
    'ID': [4, 5],
    'Name': ['Mike', 'Sarah']
})
result = pd.concat([df1, df3])
print(result)

title: Sorting Data
content:
Sorting Data in Pandas

sort_values()

Sort DataFrame by one or multiple columns using sort_values().

sort_index()

Sort DataFrame by index.

Sorting Examples
import pandas as pd

df = pd.DataFrame({
    'Name': ['John', 'Anna', 'Peter', 'Mike'],
    'Age': [25, 30, 35, 28],
    'Salary': [50000, 60000, 55000, 45000]
})

# Sort by single column
print(df.sort_values('Age'))

# Sort by multiple columns
print(df.sort_values(['Age', 'Salary']))

# Descending order
print(df.sort_values('Salary', ascending=False))

# Sort by index
df2 = df.set_index('Name')
print(df2.sort_index())

title: Pivoting & Melting
content:
Pivoting & Melting in Pandas

pivot()

Reshape data from long to wide format using pivot().

melt()

Reshape data from wide to long format using melt().

Pivot Examples
import pandas as pd

# Pivot example
df = pd.DataFrame({
    'Date': ['2023-01', '2023-01', '2023-02', '2023-02'],
    'City': ['NYC', 'LA', 'NYC', 'LA'],
    'Sales': [100, 200, 150, 250]
})

pivot_df = df.pivot(index='Date', columns='City', values='Sales')
print(pivot_df)

# Melt example
df2 = pd.DataFrame({
    'Name': ['John', 'Anna'],
    'Math': [90, 85],
    'Science': [88, 92]
})

melted = pd.melt(df2, id_vars=['Name'], 
                 var_name='Subject', 
                 value_name='Score')
print(melted)

title: Data Types & Conversion
content:
Data Types in Pandas

Checking Data Types

Use dtypes attribute to check data types of columns.

Type Conversion

Convert columns to different types using astype().

Data Type Examples
import pandas as pd

df = pd.DataFrame({
    'Name': ['John', 'Anna'],
    'Age': ['25', '30'],
    'Salary': [50000.5, 60000.0],
    'IsActive': [True, False]
})

# Check data types
print(df.dtypes)

# Convert types
df['Age'] = df['Age'].astype(int)
df['Salary'] = df['Salary'].astype(int)
df['IsActive'] = df['IsActive'].astype(str)

print(df.dtypes)

# Numeric conversion
df['Age'] = pd.to_numeric(df['Age'])
df['Salary'] = pd.to_numeric(df['Salary'])

title: Statistical Analysis
content:
Statistical Analysis with Pandas

Descriptive Statistics

Pandas provides many statistical methods: mean(), median(), std(), min(), max(), etc.

describe() & corr()

Use describe() for summary statistics and corr() for correlation.

Statistics Examples
import pandas as pd

df = pd.DataFrame({
    'Age': [25, 30, 35, 28, 32],
    'Salary': [50000, 60000, 55000, 45000, 65000],
    'Experience': [2, 5, 4, 1, 7]
})

# Basic statistics
print(df.mean())
print(df.median())
print(df.std())
print(df.min())
print(df.max())

# Summary statistics
print(df.describe())

# Correlation
print(df.corr())

title: Handling Dates
content:
Date/Time Handling in Pandas

DateTime Conversion

Convert columns to datetime using pd.to_datetime().

DateTime Features

Extract year, month, day, dayofweek from datetime columns.

DateTime Examples
import pandas as pd

df = pd.DataFrame({
    'Date': ['2023-01-01', '2023-02-01', '2023-03-01']
})

# Convert to datetime
df['Date'] = pd.to_datetime(df['Date'])

# Extract date features
df['Year'] = df['Date'].dt.year
df['Month'] = df['Date'].dt.month
df['Day'] = df['Date'].dt.day
df['DayOfWeek'] = df['Date'].dt.dayofweek

print(df)

# Date range
dates = pd.date_range('2023-01-01', periods=5, freq='D')
print(dates)

title: Exporting Data
content:
Exporting Data with Pandas

Export to CSV

Save DataFrames to CSV, Excel, JSON, and other formats.

Other Formats

Pandas supports exporting to many formats.

Export Examples
import pandas as pd

df = pd.DataFrame({
    'Name': ['John', 'Anna', 'Peter'],
    'Age': [25, 30, 35],
    'City': ['NYC', 'LA', 'Chicago']
})

# Export to CSV
df.to_csv('output.csv', index=False)

# Export to Excel
df.to_excel('output.xlsx', index=False)

# Export to JSON
df.to_json('output.json', orient='records')

# Export to HTML
df.to_html('output.html')

# Export to SQL
# df.to_sql('table_name', connection)

title: Course Summary
content:
Pandas Complete Course Summary 🎉

You Did It! 🎉

You have successfully completed the Pandas course! You now have a solid foundation in data manipulation with Python.

  • Data Structures: Series and DataFrame.
  • Data Operations: Selection, Filtering, Sorting.
  • Data Cleaning: Missing values, duplicates.
  • Analysis: Grouping, merging, statistical analysis.
  • I/O: Reading and writing various file formats.

Keep practicing Pandas! It's essential for data science and data analysis with Python!
